questions.question: Define deadlock and explain any two necessary conditions for its occurrence.
keyPoints: Deadlock is a state where processes are permanently blocked due to circular waiting
Any two conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait

questions.question: What is a safe state in the context of Banker's Algorithm? How is it different from an unsafe state?
keyPoints: Safe state: system can allocate resources to all processes in some order without deadlock
Unsafe state: no safe sequence exists, deadlock may occur
Safe state guarantees no deadlock will occur if processes request resources optimally

questions.question: Explain the Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) and how it helps in deadlock detection.
keyPoints: RAG is a directed graph with process nodes and resource nodes
Edges represent request (process→resource) and assignment (resource→process)
A cycle in RAG with single-instance resources indicates deadlock
With multiple instances, cycle is necessary but not sufficient for deadlock

questions.question: Differentiate between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance with examples.
keyPoints: Prevention: ensure one of the four Coffman conditions cannot hold (e.g., resource ordering for circular wait)
Avoidance: dynamically check requests to keep system in safe state (e.g., Banker's Algorithm)
Prevention is static, avoidance is dynamic
Prevention has less overhead than avoidance

questions.question: Describe the Wait-Die and Wound-Wait schemes for deadlock prevention in transaction processing.
keyPoints: Wait-Die: non-preemptive - younger transaction dies if requesting older's resource, older waits
Wound-Wait: preemptive - older wounds (preempts) younger if requesting younger's resource, younger waits
Both use timestamp to determine priority (older = higher priority)
Prevents circular wait by ensuring no cycle in wait-for graph

questions.question: Explain the Banker's Algorithm with a numerical example. Show how it determines whether a request can be granted.
keyPoints: Explain data structures: Available, Maximum, Allocation, Need
Explain safety algorithm: Work = Available, check if Need ≤ Work for each process
If all can finish, system is safe
Resource Request Algorithm: if Request ≤ Need and Request ≤ Available, pretend allocation and recheck safety
Provide step-by-step example with 3 processes and resource types

questions.question: Explain the deadlock detection algorithm for multiple instances of resources. How is the wait-for graph used in this context?
keyPoints: Detection algorithm: maintain Request matrix, Work = Available
For each process, check if Request ≤ Work, if so pretend completion
Continue until all processes marked or deadlock found
Wait-for graph: remove resource nodes from RAG, keep process-to-process edges
A cycle in wait-for graph indicates deadlock
Explain the algorithm steps with complexity O(m × n²)

questions.question: Discuss various methods for deadlock recovery after detection. Evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of each method.
keyPoints: Process Termination: abort all deadlocked processes or abort one at a time until deadlock breaks
Advantages: simple, clean break. Disadvantages: loss of work, may need to restart from beginning
Resource Preemption: forcibly take resources from victims and allocate to others
Advantages: may preserve more work. Disadvantages: possible starvation, data inconsistency
Rollback: return process to safe state and restart (requires checkpointing)
Selection criteria for victim: minimize cost (priority, execution time, resources held)

questions.question: Compare and contrast the four methods for handling deadlocks: Prevention, Avoidance, Detection, and Recovery.
keyPoints: Prevention: ensure one condition cannot hold - low overhead, restricts resource usage
Avoidance: use Banker's Algorithm to stay safe - moderate overhead, requires knowledge of maximum needs
Detection: periodically check for deadlock - low cost per check, but may cause damage before detection
Recovery: break deadlock after detection - requires termination or preemption, may lose work
Trade-offs between restriction, overhead, and system utilization
Most OS use combination: prevention/avoidance + detection + recovery
